skill.name: geniml
skill.description: Machine learning toolkit for genomic interval (BED) data; use it when you need to tokenize BED collections and train embeddings for regions/cells/labels, build consensus peak universes, or run similarity search and downstream ML on chromatin accessibility datasets.
skill.summary: Apply machine learning to genomic BED data for region embeddings, cell clustering, and similarity search across chromatin accessibility experiments.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 187 static analysis findings are false positives. The skill is documentation for the geniml bioinformatics toolkit. 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' findings are CLI examples in markdown files. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' findings are SHA256 metadata hashes. 'Hidden file' findings reference a legitimate cache directory path (~/.bedcache). 'Hardcoded URL' findings are software dependency links. An existing audit (geniml_audit_result_v1.json) confirms the skill is Production Ready with all gates passed and a score of 86/100.

low_findings.title: Shell command examples in documentation markdown files
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 156 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' across reference markdown files. These are CLI usage examples in code blocks showing how to use geniml, uniwig, and StarSpace commands. The files are documentation, not executable scripts. No actual command execution occurs from these files.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptographic algorithm pattern match in metadata hashes
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 21 instances of 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' in metadata JSON and markdown documentation. These are SHA256 hash values used for content integrity verification and standard bioinformatics parameter descriptions containing hash-like patterns. SHA256 is not a weak algorithm.

low_findings.title: Hidden file in home directory for caching
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged `~/.bedcache` as a hidden file in the home directory. This is a legitimate cache directory for the BBClient component that caches remote BED files for performance. It is not a hidden file access for malicious purposes.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs pointing to legitimate software repositories
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 5 hardcoded URLs. These are links to: the source GitHub repository, Facebook Research StarSpace dependency, and the geniml PyPI package. All are legitimate software dependency and source URLs for a bioinformatics toolkit.

low_findings.title: System reconnaissance pattern match in parameter descriptions
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 'System reconnaissance' at 2 locations. These are benign documentation text: filter sizes for bioinformatics peak calling (references/consensus_peaks.md:168) and an evaluation score note (geniml_audit_result_v1.json:35). No reconnaissance behavior detected.

low_findings.title: Dynamic import() expression in R integration example
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged `import("geniml.bbclient")` as a dynamic import. This is a reticulate R library call in a documentation example showing how to use geniml from R. It is an illustrative example, not executable code with injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented shell command execution via external tools
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents running external commands including geniml CLI, uniwig, cat, and StarSpace. These commands are executed by the AI tool when a user asks to run them. While legitimate for the bioinformatics workflow, an AI agent executing these commands has full system access. All commands target bioinformatics operations only.

content.user_title: Analyze genomic BED data with machine learning embeddings
content.value_statement: Genomic researchers need to extract meaningful features from large collections of BED interval files but lack tools for unsupervised representation learning. This skill guides users through the geniml toolkit to tokenize BED data, train region and cell embeddings, build consensus peak universes, and run similarity search and clustering on chromatin accessibility datasets.
actual_capabilities: Tokenize BED collections against a reference universe to create numeric features for machine learning
Train Region2Vec word2vec-style embeddings that capture similarity between genomic region sets
Build consensus peak universes using coverage cutoff, maximum likelihood, or HMM methods
Train scEmbed cell embeddings from single-cell ATAC-seq data for clustering and annotation
Evaluate embedding quality using silhouette score, Davies-Bouldin index, and other metrics
Search across region sets and metadata labels using the BEDspace joint embedding space
limitations: Requires pre-installed geniml Python package version 3.9+ and optional StarSpace binary for BEDspace features
Cannot process raw sequencing data (FASTQ, BAM) or perform variant calling; input must be BED format intervals
Embedding quality depends heavily on universe quality and tokenization parameters; may need iteration
HMM-based universe construction is computationally intensive and may be slow on large datasets

use_cases.title: Compare chromatin accessibility across experiments
use_cases.description: A bioinformatics researcher has 50 ATAC-seq BED files from different tissues and wants to cluster them by similarity without predefined labels. Use Region2Vec to tokenize against a universe and train embeddings, then evaluate cluster quality.
use_cases.target_user: Bioinformatics researcher with BED file collections

use_cases.title: Annotate single-cell ATAC-seq cell types
use_cases.description: A single-cell genomics scientist has scATAC-seq data in AnnData format and needs to cluster cells and identify cell types. Use scEmbed to train cell embeddings, run Leiden clustering and UMAP, then annotate using KNN against a reference dataset.

use_cases.title: Query genomic region sets by metadata labels
use_cases.description: A computational biologist wants to find which region sets in a database match a specific cell type label. Use BEDspace to train joint embeddings of regions and metadata, then perform label-to-region similarity search.
use_cases.target_user: Computational biologist working with annotated genomic datasets

prompt_templates.title: Quick start: Install and run basic embedding workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner wants to install geniml and run a simple end-to-end embedding pipeline on their BED files.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a folder of BED files from ATAC-seq experiments. Install geniml with ML extras, build a consensus peak universe from the combined BED files using the coverage cutoff method with cutoff 5 and merge distance 100, then tokenize the BED files against this universe and train a Region2Vec model with embedding dimension 100. Finally, evaluate the embedding quality using silhouette score.

prompt_templates.title: Single-cell ATAC-seq clustering with scEmbed
prompt_templates.scenario: A user has scATAC-seq data in h5ad format and wants to cluster cells.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a single-cell ATAC-seq dataset in AnnData format (scatac_data.h5ad). Load the data, tokenize cells against the universe file universe.bed, train an scEmbed model with embedding dimension 100 and 50 epochs, encode the cells, and add the embeddings to the AnnData object. Then run Scanpy neighbors, Leiden clustering, and UMAP visualization colored by cluster.

prompt_templates.title: Joint region and label embedding with BEDspace
prompt_templates.scenario: A user wants to perform cross-modal similarity search between genomic regions and metadata labels.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a folder of BED files with a metadata CSV containing tissue and cell type labels. Preprocess the data for StarSpace using geniml bedspace preprocess with labels cell_type and tissue, train a BEDspace model with dimension 100 and 50 epochs, calculate distances, and then search for region sets similar to the label T_cell using label-to-region search.

prompt_templates.title: Custom consensus peak universe with HMM
prompt_templates.scenario: An advanced user needs a robust reference universe using probabilistic methods.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have 100 ATAC-seq BED files and need a high-quality consensus peak universe. Build coverage tracks using uniwig with a 25bp smoothing window. Then construct a universe using the HMM method with 3 hidden states, merge distance 200, and filter size 50. After building, assess universe quality by checking region count, mean region size, and input peak coverage. Compare results with a CC method universe built from the same data.

output_examples.input: I have BED files from 5 different brain cell types. Can you help me build a universe and train embeddings to see how they cluster?
output:
Of course. I will build a consensus peak universe from your BED files, tokenize them, and train Region2Vec embeddings. Here is the recommended approach:

**Step 1 - Build the universe:** I will use the coverage cutoff method as a starting point. The universe will contain consensus peaks that appear across multiple cell types.

**Step 2 - Tokenization:** Each BED file will be converted into a token vector based on which universe peaks it overlaps.

**Step 3 - Training:** Region2Vec will learn 100-dimensional embeddings by treating each tokenized file like a document and each peak like a word.

**Expected output:** A similarity matrix showing which cell type region sets cluster together, plus silhouette scores to validate cluster quality. Similar cell types (e.g., microglia and astrocytes) should show closer embedding distances than distant types.

output_examples.input: I have scATAC-seq data from PBMCs. Cluster the cells and help me find cell type markers.
output:
I will process your scATAC-seq data through the scEmbed pipeline. Here is what to expect:

**Data pipeline:** Load the AnnData file, tokenize cells against the reference universe, train scEmbed, and generate cell embeddings.

**Clustering:** After adding embeddings to the AnnData object, I will compute a neighborhood graph and run Leiden clustering at resolution 0.5.

**Visualization:** A UMAP projection will show distinct cell populations.

**Expected output:** A UMAP plot with 6-10 clusters corresponding to expected PBMC populations (T cells, B cells, NK cells, monocytes, dendritic cells). The scEmbed embeddings should separate these populations with minimal batch effects and produce biologically meaningful clusters.

best_practices: Build a high-quality consensus peak universe before tokenization. Use a coverage cutoff that balances sensitivity and specificity (cutoff of 5 is a good starting point, adjust based on coverage depth).
Always validate tokenization coverage before training embeddings. Target more than 80 percent coverage to ensure the universe adequately represents the input regions.
Use the same universe file consistently across all preprocessing, tokenization, and analysis steps to ensure comparability between experiments.
anti_patterns: Skipping universe construction and using arbitrary reference BED files. A non-representative universe will produce poor tokenization and meaningless embeddings.
Using default parameters without adjusting for dataset size. Small datasets need lower embedding dimensions and fewer shufflings; large datasets can use higher values but require more memory.
Running BEDspace training without the StarSpace binary installed. The preprocessing step will succeed but training will fail if StarSpace is not available on the system PATH.

faq.question: What is a consensus peak universe and why do I need one?
faq.answer: A consensus peak universe is a reference set of genomic regions that appear consistently across multiple experiments. It acts as a shared vocabulary for tokenizing BED files, ensuring that all region sets use the same reference coordinates for embedding training. Without it, embeddings from different experiments cannot be compared.

faq.question: What is the difference between hard and soft tokenization?
faq.answer: Hard tokenization assigns each genomic interval to universe bins based on strict overlap criteria. Soft tokenization allows partial matches using an overlap proportion threshold. Hard tokenization is preferred for Region2Vec and scEmbed pipelines while soft tokenization is useful when dealing with noisy or imprecise peak calls.

faq.question: How do I choose the right universe construction method?
faq.answer: Start with Coverage Cutoff (CC) for quick results. Use Coverage Cutoff Flexible (CCF) when peak boundaries are uncertain. Use Maximum Likelihood (ML) for publication-level statistical rigor. Use Hidden Markov Model (HMM) only when modeling complex chromatin state patterns as it is computationally expensive.

faq.question: Can I use geniml with single-cell data from 10x Genomics?
faq.answer: Yes. scEmbed supports single-cell ATAC-seq data from 10x Genomics. You need to convert the 10x output files (barcodes, peaks, matrix) into AnnData format first. The skill provides code examples for loading 10x data using scanpy and scipy.

faq.question: What is the minimum number of BED files needed for Region2Vec training?
faq.answer: Region2Vec works best with at least 10 to 20 BED files to learn meaningful co-occurrence patterns. With fewer files, the embedding quality decreases significantly. For very small collections, consider using the BEDspace approach instead, which can incorporate metadata to improve signal.

faq.question: Does this skill work with the Claude Code CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The geniml skill works with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. When using Claude Code, the AI can execute geniml commands directly, interpret BED file contents, and visualize results. All commands documented in the skill are compatible with Claude Code's terminal execution capabilities.
